learning_objectives: Describe the shared responsibility model for IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS
Identify unique cloud security challenges compared to on-premises
Apply the CSA Cloud Controls Matrix to cloud environments

lecture_notes.introduction: Cloud computing fundamentally changes the security model. Understanding what you control versus what the provider controls is the foundation of cloud security.
key_concepts: Shared responsibility model across IaaS/PaaS/SaaS
Cloud deployment models: public, private, hybrid, multi-cloud
CSA Cloud Controls Matrix domains
Cloud-specific threats: data breaches, misconfiguration, insecure APIs
Cloud security posture management (CSPM)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Reviewing the AWS Shared Responsibility Model and auditing an AWS account's security posture using AWS Security Hub

review.value: In IaaS, the customer secures everything above the hypervisor (OS, applications, data). The provider secures the physical infrastructure and hypervisor. In SaaS, the provider secures nearly everything; the customer manages access and data.
Cloud misconfiguration (public S3 buckets, open security groups, excessive IAM permissions) is the leading cause of cloud breaches. CSPM tools continuously audit for misconfigurations.

class_schedule.topic: Identity and Access Management in the Cloud
learning_objectives: Implement least-privilege IAM policies in AWS
Configure MFA and conditional access policies
Detect and remediate excessive IAM permissions

lecture_notes.introduction: In cloud environments, identity IS the perimeter. IAM misconfigurations are the most common path to cloud compromise.
key_concepts: IAM policy structure: principal, action, resource, condition
Least-privilege IAM: policy boundaries, permission boundaries
Service accounts and workload identity
IAM Access Analyzer for detecting over-permissive policies
Cross-account access and role assumption chains
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating a least-privilege IAM policy for a Lambda function, using IAM Access Analyzer to detect external access, and implementing a permission boundary

review.value: AWS evaluates: explicit deny overrides everything, then checks for explicit allow. If neither, default deny. Organization SCPs, permission boundaries, and resource policies all participate.
Certain IAM permissions (iam:CreatePolicy, iam:AttachUserPolicy, sts:AssumeRole) enable privilege escalation paths. Must be restricted to break-glass admin accounts only.

class_schedule.topic: Cloud Network Security and Data Protection
learning_objectives: Design secure VPC architectures with proper segmentation
Implement encryption at rest and in transit for cloud data

lecture_notes.introduction: Cloud networking introduces virtual constructs that require new security thinking. Data protection in the cloud must address both the provider's responsibilities and the customer's encryption obligations.
key_concepts: VPC design: public/private subnets, NAT gateways, VPC endpoints
Security groups vs NACLs: stateful vs stateless
Encryption at rest: KMS, CMK, envelope encryption
Encryption in transit: TLS, VPN, PrivateLink
VPC Flow Logs for network monitoring
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a secure VPC with public and private subnets, configuring security groups, enabling VPC Flow Logs, and setting up S3 bucket encryption with KMS

review.value: PrivateLink endpoints keep traffic between your VPC and AWS services on the AWS backbone, never traversing the public internet. Essential for sensitive data.
Data is encrypted with a data key, and the data key is encrypted with a KMS master key. Only the encrypted data key is stored alongside the data. Decryption requires KMS access.

class_schedule.topic: Container and Serverless Security in the Cloud
learning_objectives: Secure container orchestration with EKS/GKE security policies
Implement serverless security for Lambda/Cloud Functions
Manage container image supply chain security

lecture_notes.introduction: Containers and serverless architectures introduce new security paradigms. The ephemeral nature of these workloads requires security to be built into the deployment pipeline, not bolted on afterward.
key_concepts: Kubernetes RBAC and network policies
Pod security standards: restricted, baseline, privileged
Container image scanning: ECR scanning, Trivy
Lambda security: execution role, VPC placement, layer security
Supply chain security: SBOM, image signing, admission controllers
lecture_notes.demonstration: Deploying a hardened EKS cluster with pod security standards, implementing an admission controller that rejects unsigned images, and configuring Lambda with least-privilege execution role

review.value: Kubernetes defines three levels: Privileged (unrestricted), Baseline (prevents known privilege escalations), Restricted (heavily restricted). Enforce at namespace level with admission controllers.

Lambda functions inherit their execution role's permissions. An injection vulnerability in a Lambda with broad permissions can access any AWS resource the role allows. Least-privilege execution roles are critical.

class_schedule.topic: Cloud Security Monitoring and Incident Response
learning_objectives: Configure CloudTrail, GuardDuty, and Config for security monitoring

lecture_notes.introduction: Cloud environments generate extensive telemetry. Properly configured monitoring and alerting enables detection of cloud-specific attacks like credential compromise, resource hijacking, and data exfiltration.
key_concepts: CloudTrail: API call logging for all AWS actions
GuardDuty: ML-based threat detection for cloud environments
AWS Config: configuration compliance monitoring
Cloud attack patterns: credential theft, crypto-mining, data exfiltration
Cloud IR: evidence collection, containment, recovery
lecture_notes.demonstration: Investigating a simulated GuardDuty finding of compromised credentials: tracing CloudTrail events, identifying unauthorized resource creation, and executing containment

review.value: Every API call in AWS is logged in CloudTrail. Key events: ConsoleLogin, CreateAccessKey, RunInstances, PutBucketPolicy. The source IP, user agent, and assumed role reveal the attack chain.
No physical access for evidence collection. Use API-based snapshots (EBS snapshots, S3 object versions). Containment via security group changes and IAM policy modifications, not network cable pulls.

learning_objectives: Design security controls for multi-cloud environments

lecture_notes.introduction: Most enterprises use multiple cloud providers. Consistent security across AWS, Azure, and GCP requires infrastructure-as-code and policy-as-code approaches.
key_concepts: Multi-cloud security challenges: inconsistent controls, different IAM models
Infrastructure as Code security: Terraform with tfsec scanning
Policy as Code: Open Policy Agent (OPA) for automated compliance
CSPM tools: Prisma Cloud, Wiz, Aqua for multi-cloud visibility
Compliance automation: CIS benchmarks for cloud
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing Terraform configurations with tfsec scanning to prevent misconfiguration, and OPA policies that enforce encryption and public access restrictions

review.value: Define security policies as code (OPA/Rego) that automatically evaluates infrastructure changes before deployment. Prevents misconfigurations from reaching production.
Integrate security scanning into CI/CD pipelines: tfsec in PR checks, image scanning in build, OPA in deployment. Catch issues before they reach production.

quizzes.topic: Cloud Fundamentals, IAM, and Network Security

questions.question: An AWS S3 bucket containing customer PII was found to be publicly accessible. The bucket policy allows s3:GetObject for principal '*'. Explain the chain of failures that led to this exposure, and describe three specific controls that would have prevented it.
questions.answer: Chain of failures: (1) The bucket policy explicitly grants public access ('Principal': '*'). (2) The S3 Block Public Access settings at both account and bucket level were not enabled. (3) No CSPM tool was monitoring for public bucket configurations. Prevention controls: (1) Enable S3 Block Public Access at the AWS account level, which overrides any bucket policy that grants public access. (2) AWS Config rule 's3-bucket-public-read-prohibited' that automatically flags and remediates public buckets. (3) IAM permission boundary preventing non-admin users from modifying bucket policies, combined with a review process for bucket policy changes.

rubric.key: Identifies the specific policy and account-level failures

questions.question: An IAM user has the policy: {Effect: Allow, Action: 'iam:*', Resource: '*'}. Explain the specific privilege escalation path this enables and how you would detect it.
questions.answer: This policy grants full IAM control. Escalation path: The user can create a new IAM policy with AdministratorAccess and attach it to their own user, effectively granting themselves full AWS account access. They could also create new access keys for any user, assume any role, or modify any policy. Specific actions: iam:CreatePolicy, iam:AttachUserPolicy with their own ARN, or iam:CreateAccessKey for the root account. Detection: (1) IAM Access Analyzer flags policies with 'iam:*' as overly permissive. (2) CloudTrail alerts on iam:AttachUserPolicy, iam:CreatePolicy, or iam:CreateAccessKey events from non-admin users. (3) AWS Config rule 'iam-policy-no-statements-with-admin-access' detects the policy.

quizzes.topic: Container Security, Cloud Monitoring, and Compliance

questions.question: A CloudTrail log shows the following sequence from an IAM user 'developer-jane': CreateAccessKey (for user 'admin-service'), AssumeRole (OrganizationAdminRole), CreateBucketPolicy (granting public access to a data bucket). Analyze this incident and describe the response actions.
questions.answer: Analysis: This is an IAM privilege escalation and data exfiltration attempt. developer-jane created access keys for a more privileged service account, used those keys to assume an admin role, then modified a bucket policy to expose data publicly. Response: (1) Immediate: deactivate ALL access keys for both developer-jane and admin-service accounts. (2) Revert the bucket policy to deny public access. (3) Revoke active sessions using IAM inline deny policy with aws:TokenIssueTime condition. (4) Check S3 access logs to determine if data was accessed while the bucket was public. (5) Investigate: review all developer-jane CloudTrail events for the past 90 days. Check how developer-jane obtained CreateAccessKey permission for admin-service (was this authorized?).

rubric.key: Correct analysis of the escalation chain

questions.question: Explain why a Kubernetes pod running as root with hostNetwork: true and privileged: true is a critical security risk. What pod security standard would prevent this, and how does an admission controller enforce it?
questions.answer: Risk: A root + hostNetwork + privileged pod has effectively the same access as the node itself. hostNetwork means the pod shares the node's network namespace (can sniff all node traffic, bind to any port). privileged means the container has all Linux capabilities and can access host devices. Combined with root, the pod can escape the container, access the host filesystem, and compromise the entire node and all pods on it. The 'Restricted' pod security standard prevents this by requiring: runAsNonRoot, disallowing privileged containers, dropping all capabilities, and blocking hostNetwork. An admission controller (PodSecurity admission controller or OPA Gatekeeper) intercepts pod creation API requests and rejects any pod spec that violates the namespace's security standard.

rubric.key: Identifies Restricted standard with specific requirements

major_assignments.title: Cloud Security Architecture and Implementation
major_assignments.description: Design and deploy a secure cloud architecture for a provided business scenario. Implement IAM, network security, encryption, monitoring, and compliance automation.
objectives: Design a secure cloud architecture following the shared responsibility model
Implement IAM, VPC, and encryption controls
Demonstrate detection of simulated cloud attacks
deliverables: Compliance audit report showing control effectiveness

criteria: Proper VPC segmentation and network design
Encryption at rest and in transit configured
Secure cloud architecture following best practices
Functional security controls verified through testing
Professional documentation suitable for enterprise deployment

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of cloud security concepts and platform-specific knowledge

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on cloud security configuration and monitoring exercises

major_assignments.description: Cloud security architecture design and implementation project

midterm_examination.description: Assessment covering cloud IAM, networking, and data protection

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all cloud security topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be completed in pairs. All architecture designs and reports must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Use only designated cloud accounts for exercises. Creating resources outside lab accounts or incurring unauthorized charges is prohibited.
course_policies.lab_safety: All cloud resources must be cleaned up after labs to avoid unnecessary charges. Follow the cost management guidelines provided.
